coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis and photophysics of graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmed, Bilal
Kumar, Sumeet
Ojha, Animesh K.
Hirsch, Florian
Riese, Stefan
Fischer, Ingo
coredata.dc:description: In this work, well controlled, high yield and mono-dispersed GQDs were synthesized using one pot hydrothermal method. The GQDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Raman, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy techniques. The finger prints of carbon allotropes and presence of functional groups in the GQDs were investigated by Raman and FTIR spectroscopic measurements. The life time of defect states was measured with the help of transient absorption spectroscopy. A bi-exponential decay was observed with time constants of 53 and 762 ns and assigned to a parallel deactivation from both singlet and triplet states. The GQDs showed to be an efficient photocatalyst (80%) for the photodegradation of RhG dye under the sunlight.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Time-resolved
Raman
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: BA and AKO acknowledge the Department of Science and technology (DST) , New Delhi for the financial support through the research grant SR/FTP/PS-038/2011 . AKO is thankful to the DFG (Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft) , Germany for financial support of his visit to the research group of Prof. Ingo Fischer, Institute of Physical Chemistry, Wuerzburg, Germany for three months under contract Fi575/12-1, “Initiation of Bilateral Cooperations” and for one months as a visitor of the GRK2112. Furthermore we would like to thank Christoph Lambert for his support of the transient absorption measurements and Christian Stigloher, Marie-Christine Dabauvalle and their team from the electron microscopy facility for recording the TEM images.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis steps of GQDs.
XRD patterns of GO and GQDs. The broad band around 25 ° indicates the successful generation of GQDs.

(a) Transient absorbance of GQDs excited at 355 nm, (b) delay traces at different wavelengths, (c) transient absorption spectra recorded at different delay (d) full transient absorption displayed as a two dimensional (2D-) map.
Schematic illustration of electronic transitions, following the qualitative picture outlined in Ref. [29].

Absorption spectra of aqueous solution of pure RhG dye (a), absorption spectra of RhG dye mixed with GQDs (b), photodegradation rate of RhG dye (c), and decolorization of RhG dye and its solution mixed with GQDs under sunlight (d).
Recycling test of GQDs as photocatalysts for the photodegradation of RhG dye.
Mechanism for sunlight driven photocatalytic activity of GQDs for the degradation of RhG dye molecules.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis and photophysics of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology Allahabad-211004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology Allahabad-211004

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physical and Theoretical Chemistry, University of Wuerzburg, Am-Hubland, D-97074, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Physical and Theoretical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Organic Chemistry, University of Wuerzburg, Am-Hubland, D-97074, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, well controlled, high yield and mono-dispersed GQDs were synthesized using one pot hydrothermal method. The GQDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Raman, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy techniques. The finger prints of carbon allotropes and presence of functional groups in the GQDs were investigated by Raman and FTIR spectroscopic measurements. The life time of defect states was measured with the help of transient absorption spectroscopy. A bi-exponential decay was observed with time constants of 53 and 762 ns and assigned to a parallel deactivation from both singlet and triplet states. The GQDs showed to be an efficient photocatalyst (80%) for the photodegradation of RhG dye under the sunlight.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene and its derivatives have become the subject of considerable research interest due to their unique physical and chemical properties [1,2]. Graphene itself has zero bandgap and therefore does not fluoresce [3]. In order to induce fluorescence properties in graphene, scientists usually alter the surface structure of graphene or convert it into graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [4]. GQDs have attracted interest compared to other forms of carbon based materials because of its unique properties. Compared to two dimensional (2D) and one dimensional (1D) structures of graphene, zero dimensional (0D) GQDs with the diameter less than 10 nm show stronger quantum confinement and pronounced edge effects [3]. It introduces properties that are difficult to observe in semiconductor quantum dots, such as stable photoluminescence (PL), high fluorescence quantum yield, tunable bandgap, low toxicity, bio-compatibility and solubility in water [5,6]. Due to these properties, GQDs have potential applications in the field of optoelectronics and bio-imaging [5,6].

Previous reports [4,7,8] have demonstrated that the PL emission of GQDs depends on the excitation wavelength and covers a broad spectral range. The authors of these studies have explained the excitation wavelength dependent emission of GQDs by the presence of functional group at the surface, the GQD size, and the presence of intrinsic defect levels. Steady-state PL measurements have been considered as an efficient characterization method to understand the photophysics of GQDs [9]. However, some crucial aspects are not available from steady-state methods. Time-resolved spectroscopy can reveal additional insight into the photophysics of GQDs, including the PL mechanism [10]. A better understanding of the photophysics might subsequently guide the researchers to produce GQD’s with specific properties for their applications in optoelectronic devices.

Recently, various chemical approaches for the controlled synthesis of GQDs have been improved significantly. Feng et al. [11] prepared GQDs by using N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) followed by chemical reduction through N2H4 (hydrazine hydrate). Pan et al. [12] had reported an improved hydrothermal technique for the synthesis of well crystallized GQDs using high temperature thermally reduced GO sheets as precursor. Further, GQDs were synthesized using amino acid as a reducing agent by Tetsuka et al. [13].

They have shown higher fluorescence quantum yield [3]. However, a simple water based synthetic route to produce high quality GQDs with high yield is still lacking. In this work, we report a facile in-situ hydrothermal approach for the large scale production of GQDs with tunable (ultraviolet-blue-green) luminescence properties using N2H4 as reducing agent. The hydrothermal approach provides a convenient pathway for well-controlled formation of GQDs with simultaneous reduction of GO. The synthesized samples have been characterized by different characterization techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Raman, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy. The photophysics of GQDs was investigated by transient absorption measurements and the life times of the lowest lying electronically excited states were determined. We have also explored sunlight driven photocatalytic activity of the as synthesized GQDs for RhG dye.

All chemicals were used without any further purification. All the solutions were prepared in de-ionized (DI) water. GO powder was synthesized using the procedure reported in our earlier work [14]. In a recent report [15], we have studied the production of mono-dispersed spherical GQDs (∼4 nm) using ammonia solution as both, reducing and stabilizing agent. The applications of synthesized GQDs as supercapacitor electrode and photocatalyst materials were investigated in more detail. In the present report, we followed similar synthesis steps, as reported in our recent study [15], to synthesize GQDs except using N2H4 in place of NH3. For the synthesis of GQDs, as synthesized GO powder was first dispersed in 40 ml of milli-Q distilled water at room temperature. The dispersed GO solution was then exfoliated under mild ultrasonic treatment for 1 h and subsequently 20 μl of N2H4 was added into the solution. Thereafter, the solution was transferred into a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated upto 120 °C for 12 h. The autoclave was then cooled at room temperature. The obtained precipitate was washed several times with milli-Q water and ethanol. The remaining material was then dried in an open air oven at 60 °C and the resulting black powder of GQDs was collected as the final product for further use. The size of the GQDs synthesized in the present study is relatively large compared those reported in the previous study [15]. A schematic representation of synthesis steps used for the synthesis of GQDs is presented in Fig. 1 .

The crystal phase of the synthesized samples was characterized by XRD using a powder diffractometer (Rigaku Smart Lab Diffractometer) with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Size and shape of the synthesized powder samples were determined using TEM (JEOL JEM 2100 at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV) measurements. Raman spectra of the synthesized powder samples were recorded in the spectral range 1000–2000 cm−1 at room temperature by Fourier transform Raman spectroscopy (Bruker IFS 120 h, equipped with a FRA 106 Raman module) using 1064 nm as an excitation wavelength. The laser power on the sample was 80 mW. The 180° scattering geometry was used for collecting the scattered data. For each spectrum, 200 scans were averaged. FTIR spectra of the samples were recorded using a JASCO -6100 in the spectral range 750–4000 cm−1. UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were recorded in the spectral range 250–750 nm using a JASCO (V760) UV/Vis. spectrometer. Transient absorption experiments were carried out with an Edinburgh Instrument LP 920 Laser-flash transient absorption spectrometer. The sample (1 mg) dissolved in ethanol was measured in a 1 cm quartz cuvette. It was excited by 1.2 mJ of the 355 nm third harmonic of a 10 Hz nanosecond Nd: YAG laser, the transient absorption spectra were recorded with a Xe arc lamp. The instrument response function is on the order of 14 ns. Details of the setup can be found in the literature [15].

The photocatalytic activities of the synthesized samples were determined by monitoring the photodegradation of an aqueous solution of RhG dye under sunlight. Here, RhG dye is taken as a representative water pollutant because it is one of the most commonly used organic dyes in the industry. In a typical experiment, 50 mg of the synthesized samples was dispersed as photocatalyst in 50 ml of an aqueous solution of 10−5 M RhG dye at room temperature. The suspension was sonicated for 5 min in the dark to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the mixed solution was irradiated by sunlight. The exposed solutions were taken for absorption measurement at a regular time intervals of 20 min. The absorption spectra of the samples were then recorded within the spectral range of 400–600 nm as a function of irradiation time. The residual concentration of RhG dye was calculated by monitoring the absorbance of the peak centered at ∼525 nm using the relationship:
((1) C/C0 = At/A0
where C0 and A0 are the initial concentration and absorbance of RhG dye, and C and At are the residual concentration and absorbance of dye after an irradiation time ‘t’, respectively. In the recycling experiments, the photocatalyst separated from solution was washed with ethanol and DI water before being re-dispersed in the dye solution for another cycle.

The crystal phases were determined by the XRD spectra shown in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern of GO shows a characteristic diffraction peak at 2θ = 10.58°, which corresponds to the (002) plane with an inter-planar spacing (d-spacing) of 0.83 nm. The obtained d-spacing of GO is considerably larger compared to that of 0.34 nm for pristine graphite, suggesting the successful oxidation of graphite flakes [16]. A broad diffraction peak is observed at 2θ = 25.01° corresponding to (002) plane of the GQDs. The disappearance of a diffraction peak at 2θ = 10.58° in the XRD pattern of rGO authenticates the successful reduction of GO into rGO. The larger d-spacing (0.35 nm) of rGO than that of graphite (0.34 nm) indicates the presence of oxygenated functional groups on the surface and edges of graphene sheets even after reduction [17]. The XRD pattern of GQDs as presented in Fig. 2 is identical to the XRD pattern reported in an earlier study [11].

The number of layer in GQDs is calculated by dividing the crystallite size, as determined using Scherre’s formula, with the value of interlayer spacing “d”. Thus, the value of number of layers in GQDs turns out to be 2–3 [18,19].

TEM measurements were carried out to investigate shape and size of the synthesized GQDs. The representative TEM micrographs of the synthesized samples taken at different resolutions are shown in Fig. 3 (a–b). These TEM micrographs authenticate the monodisperse and spherical nature of the GQDs. Such particles possess a large surface area which increases the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized product. The average particle size of the synthesized GQDs was estimated to be 21.4 ± 1 nm (see inset of Fig. 3). The understanding of growth behavior is very important in order to get controlled growth of GQDs. The appropriate amount and proper rate of addition of N2H4 permits to control the parameters of the precipitation process and allows to form GQDs with specific shape, size and morphology [15,17]. The surfactant molecules are bound to the surface of the synthesized products at different crystallographic faces and consequently decrease the surface energies, resulting in a controlled growth and good dispersibility of GQDs [20]. Generally, the controlled growth of nanostructured materials is determined by the nucleation process. During the synthesis, nuclei are formed through self-nucleation with a continuous supply of atoms via precursor decomposition. Instantly, the formed nuclei will start to grow in the form of nanocrystals. The shape of nuclei can have a strong effect on the final structural morphology of the nanostructures [21]. Here, it should be noted that no other reducing agent was used than N2H4, hence the formation of rGO can be attributed to the direct redox reaction between hydrazine and GO. The addition of N2H4 causes to reduce exfoliated GO, resulting reduction in the number of oxygenated functional group attached with GO. As a result, the hybridization state of carbon atom changes for sp3 (3D) to sp2 (2D) [20]. Since, the amount of N2H4 added to the GO solution is very small, it results the formation of a relatively small sized flat geometries of the carbon atoms, named as GQDs.

The Raman spectrum of GQDs is shown in Fig. 4 . Two characteristic Raman bands, D and G are expected for carbon based materials. The D band is associated with the defects present in the material, while the G band is associated with the vibrational mode of sp2 hybridized carbon atom [17]. The intensity ratio of D to G band (ID / IG) is used as a measure of disorder present in the graphitic structures [5]. The small value of ID / IG = 0.75 determined from Fig. 4 indicates that the GQDs contain more sp2 domains than sp3, which suggests a flat (2D) geometry of the GQDs.

The synthesized sample is further characterized by FTIR spectroscopy to identify functional groups present on the sample surface. The FTIR spectrum of GQDs recorded from 700 to 3500 cm−1 is shown in Fig. 5 . It shows a band located at ∼1298 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching mode of an epoxy (CO) group attached to the basal plane of GQDs [22]. The peak at ∼1602 cm–1 is due to the CC vibration of the graphene sheets [22] and confirms that a sp2 carbon structure is restored in the graphene sheets during the reduction process. The band at ∼2350 cm–1 is assigned to CO2 present in the air [23]. Interestingly no bands due to NH, OH or CO groups are detected, thus oxygen on the surface seems to be predominately present in the form of epoxy groups.

The optical properties of the GQDs are influenced by quantum confinement effects caused by the reduction in size. The effect becomes particularly pronounced when a size range of 10–25 nm is reached [24,25]. The sample studied here is right at the border of this size range. The UV–vis spectrum in the spectral range of 250–750 nm of the GQDs (mean size ∼21 nm) dispersed in ethanol is shown in Fig. 6 . It can be seen from the Fig. 6 that the GQDs exhibit a prominent absorption band at ∼275 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds of sp2 domains [24], in agreement with the restoration of an extended π-electron system. The GQDs are supposed to be composed by sp2 domains that can be regarded as a distribution of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) of different ring size. The subsequent transitions from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) in each PAH domain lead to a distribution of π-π* transitions over a large wavelength range and form a band-like feature in the UV-spectrum of GQDs that explains the broad absorption from 250 nm to 450 nm. The present result is fully consistent with a previous report [6]. A weak absorption band also appears at ∼490 nm. This band might be assigned to n - π* transition in epoxy group (CO) attached with GQDs [6]. As visible, the absorption extends further to longer wavelength side. This shoulder is assigned to absorption into defect states of the GQDs.

In order to gain more insight into the excited-state dynamics of the GQDS, the transient absorption spectra of GQDs dispersed in ethanol were recorded. The results of the transient absorption (TA) measurements of GQDs are shown in Fig. 7 (a). The time constants are extracted from the experimentally recorded decay profile by global fitting [26,27]. The result of fitting is shown in Fig. 7(a) for absorption at λprobe = 706 nm as an example. While the TA signal is given as a black line, the fit is depicted in red. For data fitting, we recorded the instrumental response function (IRF) at a wavelength close to the peak absorption line (∼750 nm) and determined a value of 14 ns. The residuals and corresponding chi squared value were used to measure the quality of fits and also used to differentiate mono and bi-exponential behaviors. The green curve shows the residuals, which describes the quality of fitting. The fitting of the decay curve yielded a bi-exponential decay with life times of 53 (τ1) and 762 (τ2) ns.

The delay traces at different wavelengths are shown in Fig. 7(b) and transient spectra at different delays are shown in Fig. 7(c). A 2D map of the complete TA spectrum is depicted in Fig. 7(d). As visible from the two traces, the shorter decay component of 53 ns is more pronounced at long probe wavelengths and contributes less in the blue part of the spectrum. A similar bi-exponential decay was observed in previously reported study on single-walled carbon nanotubes [28] wherein the authors also found a bi-exponential decay of luminescence, which was explained in terms of two closely lying defect states [DS] and [DT] within the band gap. Bi-exponential decay in C13x GQDs had also been observed previously by Mueller et al. [29]. The authors concluded that the photoexcited GQDs relax to ground state through (i) singlet states by internal conversion (IC) and (ii) triplet states via inter-system crossing (ISC). A schematic illustration of electronic transition in GQDs is shown in Fig. 8 . Thus, the GQDs should emit both, fluorescence and phosphorescence at room temperature. We assume that a similar mechanism is active here as well. In particular, τ2 = 760 ns can reasonably only be assigned to deactivation of a triplet state. After photoexcitation, rapid non-radiative deactivation sets in populating both singlet and triplet states and relaxing to S1 and T1. As pointed out by Mueller et al. [29], a higher ISC quantum yield can be expected for GQDs as compared to PAHs molecules. The bi-exponential decay in the present study would then be due to a parallel relaxation from S1 to the ground state with life time τ1 = 53 ns and from T1 to the ground state with life time τ2 = 762 ns. Note that in a sequential process τ1 should be visible as an additional rise time of the slower component. As this is not the case, the two time constants are assigned to parallel processes rather than a sequential one. When we excite the sample with 355 nm, several singlet states might be populated, as shown in Fig. 8 and partly relax to triplet states through inter-system crossing. This ISC process is too fast to be time-resolved with the employed instrument and its 14 ns IRF. While the T1 state has a broad absorption that extends over the whole probe spectrum, the S1 state has an absorption that maximizes around 700 nm. The possible electronic transitions in GQDs are schematically illustrated in Fig. 8. Most likely, intersystem crossing in graphene and GQDs is enhanced by the delocalization of π electrons at the surface of the 2D sheet, which reduces the energy gap between singlet-triplet splitting [29].

GQDs are known to be excellent photocatalysts for the degradation of organic pollutants under sunlight [30–32]. Qu et al. [30] reported the degradation of RhB using S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots. Lu et al. [31] had reported GQDs of enhanced photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of MB dye under visible light irradiation. In a study, Li et al. [32] had reported photodegradation of MB dye under visible light irradiation using water soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots. Therefore, the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized GQDs in a RhG solution was studied under sunlight irradiation. The UV–vis absorption spectra of aqueous solutions of pure RhG dye and RhG dye in the presence of GQDs were recorded for the spectral range 450–600 nm as a function of irradiation time and are shown in Fig. 9 (a–b). For the pure RhG solution, a small decrease in absorbance is observed. However, in the presence of GQDs the intensity of the characteristic absorption band of RhG centered at ∼525 nm decreases strongly with increasing irradiation time. This result indicates that the GQDs show catalytic activity under sunlight, which may be due to the absorption tail in UV–vis spectrum. The residual concentration of RhG dye as a function of irradiation time is calculated using the UV–vis absorption according to Eq. (1) and the results are presented in Fig. 9(c). It is obvious from Fig. 9(c) that the photodegradation of pure RhG dye within 80 min is quite low (∼3%), confirming that the RhG dye is photostable under our experimental conditions. The photodegradation rate of RhG dye increases significantly upon the addition of GQDs as photocatalyst. About 80% of the dye are decomposed after exposing the solution to sunlight for 80 min. The photocatalytic activity of the GQDs may be attributed to a synergistic effect between GQDs and the RhG dye. An interaction between the GQDs and dye molecules adsorbed at the surface will lead to the formation of electron (e–)–hole (h+) pairs in the GQDs followed by a charge separation in it [33]. The high surface to volume ratio of the spherical GQDs, as evidenced by TEM measurements, (Fig. 3) will also have a significant effect on the photodegradation efficiency. The GQDs provides a large number of active surface sites for the interaction with RhG molecules through noncovalent-intermolecular interactions [33]. Furthermore, the presence of localized defect levels in the GQDs may also contribute to the photocatalytic activity, because they facilitate charge separation. Thus, from the above results and discussion, it is concluded that the GQDs synthesized by our route serve as an excellent sunlight active photocatalytic material for the photodegradation of organic pollutants. The change in color of pure RhG dye and its solution mixed with GQDs as a catalyst under irradiation of sunlight is shown in Fig. 9 (d). Furthermore, recyclability is a decisive factor for the industrial applications of the catalyst. The durability of the GQDs was investigated by repeating the experiment three times with the identically prepared solution. The results obtained from this experiment are shown in Fig. 10 . The photocatalytic efficiency of the synthesized GQDs does not display a loss of activity after three cycles.

The details of the photocatalytic mechanism are schematically shown in Fig. 11 . When sunlight is incident on the aqueous solution of the RhG dye containing GQDs, excitons are formed which quickly move through the π-network and react with the oxygen dissolved in the aqueous solution by forming superoxide radicals (O2). In a solid-state picture commonly applied to photocatalysis, electrons are ejected from valence band (VB) of GQDs to the conduction band (CB) by creating a hole in the VB and thus, electron-hole pairs are generated. During this process, GQDs act as electron acceptors and thus, the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in GQDs is effectively suppressed [7]. The adsorbed RhG molecules contribute to the production of photogenerated electrons by photosensitization. Meanwhile, the positive charge present in the GQDs interact with H2O or OH− ions and form hydroxyl (OH) radicals which also degrade RhG dye molecules. The O2 and OH radicals therefore efficiently interact with the RhG dye molecules attached to the surface of the photocatalyst through Coulomb interaction and degrade it into small fragments [17,33]. The mechanism of photodegradation can be explained in terms of the following reactions [17,33]:
D y e + h ν
→
D y e
*
D y e
*
+
G Q D s
→ G Q D s
e -
+
D y e
+ •
G Q D s
e -
+
O 2
→
O 2
- •
+
G Q D s
O 2
− •
+
D y
e
+ •
→ I n t e r m e d i a t e s
o r
f i n a l
p r o d u c t s

In summary, high yield GQDs were prepared by a simple one step hydrothermal technique. XRD, TEM and Raman spectroscopy demonstrate the formation of a well-controlled morphology of GQDs. The FTIR measurement confirms the restoration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms in the graphene sheet during the reduction. Transient absorption measurements were carried out to determine the life time of the states involved in the deactivation process. A bi-exponential decay was found with the life time 53 and 762 ns. It is explained by deactivation to the ground state from the lowest-lying singlet and triplet states. The synthesized GQDs also show excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation (80%) of RhG dye under the irradiation of sunlight for a relatively shorter exposure time (80 min).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: BA and AKO acknowledge the Department of Science and technology (DST), New Delhi for the financial support through the research grant SR/FTP/PS-038/2011. AKO is thankful to the DFG (Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft), Germany for financial support of his visit to the research group of Prof. Ingo Fischer, Institute of Physical Chemistry, Wuerzburg, Germany for three months under contract Fi575/12-1, “Initiation of Bilateral Cooperations” and for one months as a visitor of the GRK2112. Furthermore we would like to thank Christoph Lambert for his support of the transient absorption measurements and Christian Stigloher, Marie-Christine Dabauvalle and their team from the electron microscopy facility for recording the TEM images.
